data.title: Corporate Stock Buybacks - Do They Affect Markets?
data.description:
Fisher Investments recently wrote an interesting article asking whether corporate stock buybacks affect markets. Here is their conclusion:

“Yes and no? Stocks move on supply and demand. Stock buybacks, where a company buys and takes shares off the market, theoretically reduce supply. They can also raise earnings per share, thus rewarding shareholders. So, all else equal and on paper, stock buybacks are bullish. But reality, as always, is more complicated. Buybacks are just one factor affecting supply. There are others, and demand matters, too. They may not reduce supply if they merely offset secondary issuances, like employee stock awards. Often, buybacks merely ‘sterilize’ new issuance. Other negative (or less bullish) fundamental factors might matter more in pricing, lowering demand even as supply shrinks. So buybacks are a factor, but not the factor.“

While the statement is mostly correct, I am unsure if they looked at the actual impact that corporate stock buybacks have on the market. We have discussed this topic and the past misstatements of corporate stock buybacks. Here is a listing for more background.

  1. They are not a return of capital to shareholders; dividends are.
  2. Corporate stock buybacks are the worst use of cash.
  3. It is a benefit that almost entirely benefits corporate insiders.

But, without rehashing the many problems of corporate stock buybacks, let’s focus on these transactions’ impact on the overall market.

As of May 2025, corporate stock buyback authorizations are on track to eclipse $1.35 trillion this year, with more than $1 trillion executed. This will exceed any other year in the market since the turn of the century. Such should be unsurprising with Apple (AAPL) announcing an additional $100 billion and Google (GOOGL) adding another $70 billion to their programs (those two programs will account for 12% of the total alone).

The data should lead one to question why corporate stock buybacks have grown steadily since the turn of the century. Such is particularly the case when the overreliance on buybacks at non-accretive valuations to boost stock prices has become commonplace. Such a statement undermines the fallacy that corporate stock buybacks are solely a return of capital to shareholders. For example, Apple’s $110 billion buyback plan in 2024 raised questions among some investors about whether the company focused too much on immediate stock price increases rather than on investments that could drive long-term value. That statement should not be overlooked, given that 5-year annualized revenue growth has been flat since 2018. (Chart courtesy of SimpleVisor.com)

If corporate stock buybacks are not a significant factor in increasing stock prices, why do companies engage in them so heavily? Why not just let market dynamics carry the load? The reason is simplistic to understand.

“Corporate executives give several reasons for stock buybacks but none of them has close to the explanatory power of this simple truth: Stock-based instruments make up the majority of their pay and in the short-term buybacks drive up stock prices.” – Financial Times.

So, how much of a factor are buybacks?

Are Buybacks An Important Factor

It is a pretty easy task to see whether or not corporate stock buybacks influence stock prices. As we penned last year, the impact of buybacks extends beyond individual companies. Since 2000, net corporate buybacks have accounted for 100% of the equity market’s net asset purchases — a reflection of the diminished participation from pensions, mutual funds, and individual investors:

  • Net Flow: +$5.2 trillion
  • Pensions & Mutual Funds: –$2.7 trillion
  • Households & Foreign Investors: +$2.4 trillion
  • Corporations (Buybacks): +$5.5 trillion

In other words, without corporate stock buybacks, the stock market would be roughly 30% lower today.

“The chart below via Pavilion Global Markets shows the impact stock buybacks have had on the market over the last decade. The decomposition of returns for the S&P 500 breaks down as follows:“

  • 6.1% from multiple expansions (21% at Peak),
  • 57.3% from earnings (31.4% at Peak),
  • 9.1% from dividends (7.1% at Peak), and
  • 27% from share buybacks (40.5% at Peak)

Yes, buybacks are that important.

However, to Fisher’s question directly, there is more than just a minor correlation between corporate stock buybacks and the market. The chart below overlays the 4-week change in stock buybacks versus the 4-week change in the S&P 500 index. It is worth noting that before 1982, the SEC considered share buybacks an illegal form of market manipulation. (In 1982, the SEC adopted Rule 10b-18, which provided "safe harbor" from liability of market manipulation. In other words, the SEC recognizes that buybacks manipulate the financial markets but provided a “shield” to corporations.)

The chart above is complex due to the large amount of data. The chart below is from 2021 to present, where changes to buybacks (increase or decrease) significantly impact changes to stock prices. It is worth noting that the nearly 20% decline in April was exacerbated by the sharp reversal in buyback activity and vice versa.

While Fisher suggests that buybacks have little to do with market movements, a high correlation exists between the 4-week percentage change in buybacks and the stock market. More importantly, since the act of share repurchases provides a buyer for those shares, the .85 correlation between the two suggests this is more than just a casual relationship.

But yes, Fisher is correct, other factors support higher asset prices.

Buybacks Affect More Than Just Prices

In 2023, Jason Zweig penned an article for the WSJ stating:

“Over the past five years, according to S&P Dow Jones Indices, big U.S. companies have spent $3.9 trillion repurchasing their own stock. Buybacks are neither bad nor good. They are simply a tool. Just as you can use a hammer either to build a house or knock one down, buybacks are useful in the right corporate hands and dangerous in the wrong ones.“ – Jason Zweig, WSJ

That is a fair statement. The impact of share buybacks is vital to manufacturing earnings growth, since we measure earnings on a per-share basis. In other words, if you reduce the number of shares outstanding, corporate earnings “per share” improve, as shown below.

As discussed previously, the annual rate of change in earnings growth is one of the best predictors of forward stock market returns.

However, investors must be cautious about understanding the impact of buybacks on earnings when investing in companies. As Warren Buffett noted:

Finally, an important warning: Even the operating earnings figure we favor can easily be manipulated by managers who wish to do so. Such tampering is often considered sophisticated by CEOs, directors and their advisors. Reporters and analysts embrace its existence as well. Beating ‘expectations’ is heralded as a managerial triumph. That activity is disgusting. It requires no talent to manipulate numbers: Only a deep desire to deceive is required. ‘Bold, imaginative accounting,’ as a CEO once described his deception to me, has become one of the shames of capitalism.“

Why would CEOs want to manipulate earnings? Unsurprisingly, a WSJ survey of CFOs found that 93% pointed to “influence on stock price” and “outside pressure” as the reasons for manipulating earnings figures.

Of course, one misnomer is that corporate CEOs execute buybacks when they believe the stock is undervalued. However, the reality is quite the opposite, and they tend to execute share repurchases when their current optimism is elevated. When share prices decline, and buybacks could be done at accretive prices, there is little incentive to do so.

Conclusion

The evidence is clear: corporate stock buybacks are not a marginal force in markets — they are central to the mechanics of price inflation. When buybacks account for the entire net demand for equities over the last two decades, it’s hard to argue they’re simply "a" factor. They aren’t just reducing supply on paper — they are the demand. Without them, equity valuations would look very different.

But what’s more concerning is the why. Despite the popular narrative that buybacks return capital to shareholders, the data and behavior of corporate management tell a different story. Buybacks overwhelmingly inflate earnings per share and boost short-term stock prices, which are tied directly to executive compensation. That incentive skews the timing and intent of buyback programs away from long-term value creation and toward short-term financial engineering.

To Fisher’s credit, markets are complex. Demand, sentiment, interest rates, and macroeconomic factors all matter. However, dismissing buybacks as one variable among many overlooks just how much they dominate equity flows. Their influence is measurable, intentional, and reinforced by corporate leadership’s financial incentives.

The question for investors is not whether buybacks matter — they do. The question is whether they’re being used to create real value or mask its absence

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Get Into GitLab
data.description:
Thesis

This article focuses on GitLab (GTLB) a smaller SaaS company that may interest investors with high-risk tolerances. The article explains what GitLab does, its “special sauce”, operating metrics and evidence that it’s executing well with artificial intelligence. I also focus on a few unique risks and mitigants. Lastly, I seek to value the company using specialized metrics such as the Rule of 40.

Introduction

A while back, I wrote an article “Screening for the best SaaS companies” in which I presented and recommended several enterprise-software companies. Those of you that are familiar with the space will likely recognize and agree that these are true leaders. However, people often ask me about the other six dozen names that rarely appear in the financial press. I typically give the same answer I give to those investing in cryptocurrencies, which is that you should avoid smaller names unless you have a high-risk tolerance, already own the largest companies, have done your own research and want to shift your portfolio up the “efficient frontier.”

Adding GitLab to a portfolio’s Efficient Frontier

GitLab is one such name as it’s smaller and riskier than the better-known SaaS names. This higher risk can be determined by examining GTLB’s beta and revenue scale (<$1Bn). A stock’s Standard Deviation or Beta are considered measures of risk (X-axis) in the above chart.

Business description

I often find SaaS companies difficult to understand, so I will explain GitLab’s business in layman’s terms. The company is a digital workspace that helps teams (especially in large enterprises) safely organize and work together on projects (mostly software). GitLab provides a central repository to code and keep track of changes. Users can even make copies of the project or carve-outs to “play in a sandbox.” Once a user is finished with a software module, GitLab can automatically test it to ensure it’s working properly.

There’s also a project component of GitLab that’s similar to what I described in my SA article on monday.com (MNDY). For example, GitLab provides “scrum boards” in which a team leader can assign & track tasks, timelines, milestones, documentation and charting & reporting. The key difference between GitLab and Monday.com is that MNDY is more general while GitLab is marketed to more technical software-intensive teams.

Industry Size & Growth

The global DevOps software market is expected to grow from $11.5Bn in 2023 to $66Bn by 2033. This equates to a CAGR of over 19%. The Generative AI niche within this market is$2Bn and growing at a 38% CAGR.

Largest players are the Mag-7 (Microsoft (MSFT)/GitHub, Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN)) and more focused companies such as Atlassian (TEAM) JFrog (FROG) Harness (privately owned) and to a lesser degree Monday.com. Large horizontal SaaS companies such as Salesforce (CRM) also have DevOp businesses.

Business Position

GitLab rates very high in Gartner’s Magic Quadrant for DevOps Software, but a bit lower in G2’s ranking (chart below), especially amongst larger corporations.

Management Rating

I typically favor founder-led SaaS companies, which was the case for GitLab until this year when Bill Staples took over this year for “Sid” who stepped down. GitLab’s culture is highly rated by both Comparably (see below) and Glassdoor. However, note that Mr. Staples’ approval rating (54%) is troublingly low on Glassdoor. Another red-flag is that there’s a new CRO (Chief Revenue Officer) so the marketing teams may witness higher turnover as the CRO changes strategy. For example, if the CRO decides to shift from a “self-serve” model to a dedicated sales team targeting large corporations, GitLab’s 91% gross profit margin could be at risk.

Secret Sauce

Every company in the SaaS space talks about integrating Artificial Intelligence (“AI”), but GitLab seems to be executing better. Rather than just using an AI copilot to summarize meetings and generate issue descriptions, GitLab uses AI in an integrative way. In fact, GitLab has several tools and agents for all of the testing, etc. that I described under the Business Description.

GitLab’s recent quarter (4Q ’25 ended January) slightly missed estimates, especially FY ’26 guidances, which was on the conservative side (probably related to the new CEO and CRO). A discussion of 4Q is out of scope to this article, but I do recommend reading Gary Alexander’s piece.

I see anecdotal evidence that GitLab has staying power with customers such as:

  • the number of customers that are doing large deals (>$100k) is growing faster than its total customer base (“Base Customers” on chart)
  • It’s AI tool, called GitLab Duo, has been gaining traction amongst customers.
  • “cRPO” revenues are growing at a strong rate, meaning expected, but unrecognized revenues are growing at a healthy-clip compared to historical revenue growth.
  • The more expensive "Ultimate" subscription plan, which is a comprehensive package offered to large organizations, is selling well and is now accounting for 50% of ARR (annualized recurring revenue) compared to 46% three quarters earlier.
  • Net Dollar Retention has begun to stabilize at 123% (bar-chart) and is above its peers
  • Amazon Web Services has expanded its relationship with GTLB including AWS’ bundled offering of GitLab Duo

Risks

GitLab is a smaller company compared to its direct competitors such as Microsoft (MSFT) which owns GitHub and Atlassian (TEAM). It also has lower and inconsistent Free Cash Flow margins and “Rule of 40” numbers compared to those companies. These are backwards-looking metrics and I expect they will improve going forward. For example, the FCF margin in the chart below has been very volatile and the Rule of 40 was just 20% during the TTM period. However, as SaaS companies age, their business model’s Operating Leverage typically allows them to reach that 40% hurdle-rate.

One of the reasons why GitLab’s Rule of 40 is so low is due to its young age (11 years in total including about 4 years as a publicly traded company. Again, the Rule of 40 numbers age well - like a fine-wine. If GitLab surpasses 40%, it’ll be ranked in the top one-third of all SaaS companies, albeit at the bottom of that sub-list (see bar-chart).

Valuation Analysis

The best method to value SaaS companies is examining EV/NTM Revenues (i.e., EV to forward Revenues). Analysts’ estimates for GitLab’s revenue growth approximate mid-20s percentage growth and most estimates have been close to that mean. The less certain estimates pertain to FCF Margin, of which will determine whether GitLab reaches the Rule of 40 hurdle rate. If it does, then I believe GTLB is currently trading at a discount to the Regression Line in the first chart below (EV multiple vs. Rule of 40). I also examined GTLB’s EV multiple vs. forward revenue growth rate, and it too, trades at a discount (second chart below).

Conclusion

I recommend investors who are looking to shift their portfolio up the Efficient Frontier to purchase shares of GTLB (rated “ Strong Buy”). The shares could rise sharply if management continues executing and if global liquidity (e.g., M2 Money Supply) continues expanding. There is also the chance that Amazon could acquire GitLab given its growing relationship with Amazon AWS.
